coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot nanosensors for ultra-low level, rapid assay of mercury ions synthesized from an aquatic weed, Typha angustata Bory (Patera)
coredata.dc:creator: Samota, Sharmistha
Tewatia, Preeti
Rani, Reetu
Chakraverty, Suvankar
Kaushik, Anupama
coredata.dc:description: A wild plant, Typha angustata Bory (Patera) was used as a precursor for synthesizing nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) using hydrothermal method. The objective was to use an aquatic weed to develop an extremely sensitive sensor for fluorescence assay of mercury ions and cysteine in aqueous medium. N, S-CDs exhibited an unprecedented quantum yield (QY) of 83 %, not reported previously. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs substantially quenched in presence of Hg2+ ions, without being influenced with presence of interfering ions. The fluorescence intensity was regained absolutely in presence of cysteine, even after 10 cycles demonstrating exceptional reusability. Owing to extraordinary QY, the sensor exhibited ultra-low limits of detection (LOD) of 3.1 nM and 8 nM for Hg2+ and cysteine, respectively indicating extraordinary efficacy of the sensor, also confirmed using XPS studies. At neutral pH and 2 min' incubation time, the linear detection ranges for Hg2+ ions and cysteine were 0.01–60 μM and 0.05–12.5 μM, respectively. Fluorescence life decay studies exhibited that fluorescence quenching was static. Satisfactory recoverability in the range 95–102 % for real water samples, indicated high reliability and accuracy for detection of Hg2+ ions. The synthesized sensor presented a potential for facile, fast and reliable platform for detection of Hg2+ ions and cysteine for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot nanosensors for ultra-low level, rapid assay of mercury ions synthesized from an aquatic weed, Typha angustata Bory (Patera)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A wild plant, Typha angustata Bory (Patera) was used as a precursor for synthesizing nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) using hydrothermal method. The objective was to use an aquatic weed to develop an extremely sensitive sensor for fluorescence assay of mercury ions and cysteine in aqueous medium. N, S-CDs exhibited an unprecedented quantum yield (QY) of 83 %, not reported previously. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs substantially quenched in presence of Hg2+ ions, without being influenced with presence of interfering ions. The fluorescence intensity was regained absolutely in presence of cysteine, even after 10 cycles demonstrating exceptional reusability. Owing to extraordinary QY, the sensor exhibited ultra-low limits of detection (LOD) of 3.1 nM and 8 nM for Hg2+ and cysteine, respectively indicating extraordinary efficacy of the sensor, also confirmed using XPS studies. At neutral pH and 2 min' incubation time, the linear detection ranges for Hg2+ ions and cysteine were 0.01–60 μM and 0.05–12.5 μM, respectively. Fluorescence life decay studies exhibited that fluorescence quenching was static. Satisfactory recoverability in the range 95–102 % for real water samples, indicated high reliability and accuracy for detection of Hg2+ ions. The synthesized sensor presented a potential for facile, fast and reliable platform for detection of Hg2+ ions and cysteine for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization and exponential growth in consumer market has led to release of hazardous pollutants including heavy metal ions which are extremely toxic and are extremely detrimental to environment and human health. As one of the most pernicious heavy metal ions, mercury is prevalent in aqueous environment through geogenic and anthropogenic activities like coal power plants, galvanization units, tanning industries, mining, discharge of municipal waste, and combustion of fuels. Mercury and its compounds are graded as “priority hazardous substances” owing to their toxic nature, long residence time and bioaccumulation in food chain [1]. The World Health Organization (WHO) considers mercury (Hg2+) to be one of the most dangerous water pollutants due to its numerous toxicological effects like neurological damage, renal dysfunction, gastrointestinal disorders and impairment of cognitive and motion functions, even at trace levels [2–4]. Owing to the hazards associated with its presence in water bodies, it is imperative to develop accurate, rapid, economical and user-friendly methods for its detection.

Amid numerous amino acids produced by human body, cysteine is a thiol containing natural amino acid, which plays an essential biological role of synthesis of proteins via di-sulphide linkages, detoxification and metabolism. Cysteine is one of the three amino acids needed for the synthesis of GSH, an intracellular non-protein thiol critical in control of redox homeostasis and oxidative stress [5]. Abnormal level of cysteine in organisms may lead to chronic diseases like hemolytic anemia, skin lesions, autism, myalgic encephalomyelitis, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and cardiovascular diseases, hair depigmentation, liver damage and oxidative stress [6–8]. Reliable detection of cysteine concentration in the body would thus enable to analyze its biological functions and identification of associated disease.

Many methods for detecting Hg2+ and cysteine have been reported including capillary electrophoresis [9], atomic emission spectroscopy, atomic absorption spectroscopy [10], photo colorimetry [11,12], electrochemical detection [13,14], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy and surface enhanced Raman scattering. However, most of the analytical methods either require expensive equipment, tedious pretreatment methods and complex operations limiting their application for on-site analysis. [15,16]. In contrast, fluorescence sensors owing to their rapid response, facile usage, and exceptional sensitivity, inexpensive and expedient procedure are gaining immense attention for metal ion detection [17,18].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have garnered substantial research interest due to numerous advantages like simple synthetic routes, good water dispersibility, exceptional fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, high stability, low photo-bleaching, low cytotoxicity, ease of functionalization and non-flickering fluorescence emission in comparison to metal-based quantum dots [19–26]. Despite the fact that fluorescent CDs have many distinctive characteristics, their low quantum yield (QY) is their prime limitation. Recently, it has been discovered that doping CDs with heteroatoms such as sulfur, nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus can significantly improve their QY [27,28]. N-doped CDs are reported to show blue luminescence with short lifetimes, high pH sensitivity and better stability at high salt concentrations [29]. The enhanced fluorescent properties of CDs arise because N and C atoms are similar in size and structure and N have five valence electrons, which can bond with other C atoms. Therefore, N-doping increases the surface-state defects of CDs and improves their electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity by providing many reactive groups (OH, COOH, NC, CN, NH2). However, the heteroatom content and quantum yield of CDs derived from different bio mass materials are extremely low, making practical application of CDs challenging [30]. As a result, large-scale production of biomass-derived CDs with high QY is subject of great interest [31]. Various synthetic approaches for producing CDs have been widely reported over the last decade, including arc discharge, electrochemical synthesis [32,33], acid oxidation [34], microwave-assisted synthesis [25], hydro/solvothermal [35] and pyrolysis [36] methods. Amongst all, hydrothermal synthesis is a “green” route to synthesize CDs using lignocellulosic biomass as a carbon source, besides being economical and nontoxic [37].

In this work, a novel carbon dot-based fluorescence sensor was developed from an aquatic weed, Typha angustata Bory, commonly known as Patera for ultra-sensitive assay of Hg2+ ions and cysteine. Patera is a perennial plant widely grown in waterlogged areas and is a popularly used medicinal plant by local population. Hydrothermal synthesis was used to make nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs (N, S-CDs) which were highly sensitive towards Hg2+ ions in the presence of interfering ions due to specific interactions with thiol group. Thus the novelty of the work includes use of unexplored aquatic weed, Typha angustata Bory for preparing quantum dots, unprecedented fluorescence quantum yield of the order 83 % and exceptionally low limit of detection of 3.1 nM and 8 nM for Hg2+ and cysteine, respectively.

Typha angustata Bory (Patera) was obtained from local fields. Magnesium Chloride (MgCl2), Cobalt Chloride (CoCl2), Mercuric Acetate (HgC4H6O4), Iron Sulphate (Fe2(SO4)3), Cadmium Chloride (CdCl2), Lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), Copper sulphate (CuSO4) and Silver Chloride (AgCl3), were purchased from LOBA Chemie Pvt. Ltd., India. Eight amino acids including histidine, lysine, methionine, cysteine, arginine, glutathione, glycine and glutamine were purchased from Merck, Germany. Thiourea was obtained from LOBA Chemie Pvt. Ltd., India, while Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (AR grade) were procured from Ranbaxy Fine Chemicals Ltd., India and Ranken, India, respectively. No additional purification was done on any chemical used in the studies. Throughout the procedure, deionized water was utilized.

Hydrothermal method is one of the extensively used green method for the synthesis of CDs owing to its relatively mild operating conditions (synthesis temperatures < 300 °C), single step procedure, inexpensiveness, convenience and environmental friendliness without using additional chemicals. The highly fluorescent N, S-CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using Patera as a carbon precursor and thiourea as nitrogen and sulfur source, respectively. The Patera stems were cut into 3–4 cm size, carefully rinsed with ultrapure water and were dried in sun to remove superficial impurities. The pieces were mechanically ground into fine powder. To synthesize N, S-CDs, 1 g Patera powder and 0.5 g thiourea were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water and were transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 10 h as shown in Scheme 1 . Once the reaction products are cooled, they were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the sediment. Finally, the supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h (MWCO: 1kD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of process parameters for the synthesis of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the course of hydrothermal treatment, the natural plant material goes through series of stages including dehydration, polymerization and carbonization. Various parameters like reaction time and hydrothermal temperature have substantial effect on the quantum yield of CDs and thus affects the fluorescence intensity of CDs. To achieve the CDs with high fluorescence intensity, both hydrothermal temperature and time were optimized. As evident from Fig. 1(a) and (b), the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs was maximum at 10 h and 200 °C. Lower hydrothermal temperature and lower reaction time yields lower quantum yield owing to insufficient carbonization while further increase in temperature beyond 200 oC and reaction time >10 h also results in lower quantum yield due to reduction in surface functional sites [38,39].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed for N, S-CDs in the range 400–4000 cm−1 using a Perkin Elmer 400 FTIR spectrophotometer to confirm the doping of nitrogen and sulfur on the surface of CDs. The samples were pulverised and mixed with KBr in a ratio of 1:99 to make the pellets for FTIR. Philips PAN Analytical XPERT-PRO X-Ray Diffractometer with CuKα radiations (λ = 0.154060 nm) with 40 kV voltage and 40 mA intensity were used to assess the crystallinity of CDs. The size and morphology of N, S-CDs were scrutinized using a High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) model Hitachi-2100. A well homogenised dilute aqueous sample of CDs was deposited on the carbon-coated grid and dried at room temperature. Images were captured at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. Quantum yield of N, S-CDs was enumerated using Eq. (1) with quinine sulphate as a standard reference.
(1)
Qx = Qstd
Ix Istd
Astd Ax
ƞ std
ƞ x
ƞ std
ƞ x
where η, I, A and Q represented the refractive index of the solvent, integral area of fluorescence intensity, absorbance and quantum yield, respectively. The subscript std. represents the standard reference, while x represents N, S-CDs.

Hitachi Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (F-7000) with 1 cm quartz cell was used for fluorescent measurements. At room temperature, the detection of Hg2+ ions was carried out in an aqueous solution. To eliminate the influence of pH on the fluorescence of N, S-CDs, all the sensing assays were conducted in 1 mL phosphate buffered solution. Using the standard addition procedure, 15 μL of N, S-CDs were diluted to 1 mL with phosphate buffer. A 1000 ppm stock solution of Hg2+ ions was prepared by dissolving mercury acetate in distilled water and a series Hg2+ aqueous solutions of different concentrations ranging (0.01 to 60 μM) were prepared by diluting the prepared 1000 ppm stock solution. The quenching fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs in presence of Hg2+ was then investigated at different concentrations. Then, 1 mL of phosphate buffered solution was added to these solutions respectively. After shaking the mixtures into homogeneous solutions for 2 min, their fluorescence spectra were recorded (excited at 320 nm). The analytical signal was the decrease in fluorescence emission intensity at 320 nm when compared to a blank solution.

For the selectivity studies, the fluorescent intensity of N, S-CDs was investigated in the presence of various cations i.e. Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, As3+, Ag3+, Cd2+, Mg2+ at different concentration prepared by diluting their 1000 ppm stock solution. N, S-CDs fluorescence intensities were determined in the presence (F) and absence (F0) of interference. Furthermore, potential interference was investigated in order to investigate cross reactivity. The combination of Hg2+ and other metal ions was created at the same concentration level and with a content multiple times that of mercury. After a 2-min incubation period, the intensity of the fluorescence was recorded. Competitive investigations in the presence of several amino acids were used to determine the selectivity towards cysteine. A typical competitive experiment solution of N, S-CDs + Hg2+ and other amino acids (100 μM) was mixed with cysteine (100 μM), and variations in their fluorescence spectra were observed. To assess sensitivity of cysteine, a mixed solution of 60 μM Hg2+ and 15 μL of N, S-CDs was prepared at pH 7 and sonicated for 2 min. The mixture was then used for detection of cysteine in a detection range of 0.05 to 12.5 μΜ and their fluorescence spectra were observed under the same conditions. The reversibility of the “on–off–on” fluorescence sensor was next investigated by alternately introducing Hg2+ ions and cysteine. All experiments were studied at room temperature, with excitation and emission wavelengths set at 320 and 410 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercury ions detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Presence of mercury in real water samples was analyzed to validate the proposed method. Laboratory tap water and lake water were taken and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove solid suspensions and other impurities before the experiment. The filtered water samples were spiked with various concentrations of Hg2+ (0.5 μM‑20 μM) and unknown concentration was evaluated from the regression line obtained from decrease of fluorescence intensity as a function of Hg2+ concentration.

A high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to analyze the morphology of N, S-CDs. As evident from Fig. 2 (a&b), the synthesized CDs are visible as spherical dots with good dispersibility in water. Histogram for the nanoparticle size distribution [Fig. 2(b) inset], evaluated by randomly taking around 150 nanoparticles, unveiled average diameter of N,S-CDs ranging from 2 to 7 nm with average particles size of 5 ± 0.45 nm. The HRTEM image exhibited the lattice spacing of 0.26 nm [Fig. 2(a) inset], which corresponds to the (020) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon [40]. An X-ray diffractometer was used to characterize the CDs' lattice structure, as shown in Fig. 2(c), which shows a comparatively strong yet broad peak at 2θ = 24.3°, which corresponds to (002) plane of graphite structure due to existence of amorphous carbon, clearly indicating complete carbonization of precursor Patera stems [41]. The broadness of the peak may be due to presence of highly disordered or amorphous carbon. Using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, quantum yield (QY) = 0.54) as a reference, the fluorescence-QY of the N, S-CDs in aqueous solution was found to be 83 %, which is substantially greater [42,43] than that of previously reported CDs synthesized from biomass materials as carbon sources. We hypothesized that the considerable increase in fluorescence-QY of the N, S-CDs is due to the abundance of surface defects and functional groups adorned on the N, S-CDs.

FTIR spectrum was used to determine the functional groups on the prepared N, S-CDs. As evident from Fig. 2(d), typical bands for aromatic carbon clusters are visible with sp2 structure. The characteristic stretching vibrations of OH are visible at 3450 cm−1 while stretching vibrations for NH groups are responsible for the weak bands at 3220 cm−1. Small absorption band around 2925 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH stretching vibrations from sp3 carbon while out of plane bending vibrations of aromatic CH are visible 870 cm−1 [44,45]. The CO groups are represented by the strong absorption band at 2030 cm−1. There are typical tensile vibrational modes for CN and CN heterocycles are evident at 1415 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 [46]. The band at 1142 cm−1 is mainly due to CS and CN functional groups [47]. The FTIR spectra confirms that there is abundant nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen containing active groups on the surface of CDs which improve their water solubility, stability and optical properties.

The UV–VIS absorption spectrum and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of N, S-CDs are shown in Fig. 3(a). The UV absorption spectra of N, S-CDs exhibit two distinct characteristic peaks corresponding to 270 nm and 320 nm with the end prolonging into visible range. The π‑π* electronic transition of C = C resulted in peak at 270 nm, which do not consequent any fluorescence signal, while the n‑π* electronic transition of CO caused peak at 320 nm which result in a strong fluorescence signal This is due to the fact that the excited carriers are trapped by localized surface states, resulting in intense fluorescence emission [48]. To characterize the optical properties of N, S-CDs, broad emission spectrum was measured at varying excitation wavelength as exhibited in Fig. 3(b). When CDs are excited at shorter wavelength, it provides higher energy thus permitting electrons to be transmitted at higher vibrational level, thus reducing emission energy, increasing stokes shifts and reducing the fluorescence intensity. The peak position shifted to a greater wavelength as the excitation wavelength ranged from 260 nm to 420 nm. This may be due to the particle size distribution and complex surface excited states of N, S-CDs and varying distribution of emission sites on every particle of CDs [49]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for N and S doped CDs were 320 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the optical stability of N, S-CDs was also investigated. Initially for a month N, S-CDs exhibited a reasonable fluorescence stability, thereafter, there was slight decrease after storage for 3 months at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 3(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of N, S-CDs are well known to be correlated to their surface state. The fluorescent characteristics of N, S-CDs may be affected by pH changes as pH effects their state of protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs in basic and acidic medium. To confirm this, the relationship between pH and N, S-CDs fluorescence intensity was investigated from pH ranging from 1 to 12. Fig. 3(d) exhibits the change in fluorescence intensity with pH. As the pH changes from 1 to 7, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increased significantly. At strong acidic conditions, protonation ensues at surface emission sites, reducing the probability of electron radiation transition, leading to reduction of fluorescence intensity [50]. The fluorescence intensity achieves a maximum and relatively steady value as pH is raised to 7. Further, in basic conditions, the intensity of the fluorescence decreased gradually. This is probably because CDs serve as both electron donors and acceptors, and the adjacent environmental OH− ions and H+ ions may contribute to electron transfer. The variation in fluorescence intensity of CDs suggest its applicability in the neutral medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N, S-CDs as fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and cysteine
Selectivity and interference studies for detection of Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs synthesized from patera stems contain many surface functional groups including NH2, OH and SH, which apart from enhancing the water solubility, show strong affinity for Hg2+ ions in aqueous medium. Ten frequently existing metal ions in water bodies including As2+, Ag3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Mg2+ were selected to evaluate the selectivity of N, S-CDs and to assess the effect on fluorescence intensity in the presence of various interfering ions. As shown in Fig. 4(a), amongst all metal ions evaluated Hg2+ showed the highest selectivity towards quenching of fluorescence signal of CDs. To study the effect of interfering coexisting ions on fluorescence quenching, different metals were added along with Hg2+ ions and the fluorescence signal of CDs at 410 nm was observed. As evident in Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence emission intensity scarcely changed in the presence of other interfering ions suggesting that CDs are highly selective for Hg2+ ions and there is no effect of coexisting ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The pH and incubation time were optimized to improve the detection performance of the CD sensor for Hg2+. Fig. 4(c) illustrates the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity ratio increases as pH changes from 1 to 7, reaches a maximum at neutral pH and subsequently decreases. under alkaline conditions. This may be due to the fact that most of the functional groups on the surface of CDs are protonated at acidic pH, and the rivalry between H+ and Hg2+ reduces the number of active sites available on the CDs. Hg2+ interacts with OH− to generate Hg(OH)2, which can easily be transformed into a HgO precipitate under alkaline circumstances. As a result, acidic or alkaline conditions are incompatible with the detection of mercury ions. The neutral pH was preferred for the optimal detection as it is similar to the pH of physiological atmospheres. The influence of incubation time on the reaction system was also examined to provide a reliable signal. The Fig. 4(d) indicates that detection takes only 2 min, during which time the fluorescence quenching effect increases and subsequently stabilizes. As a result, the optimal incubation time was taken as 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of detection limit for Hg2+ ions using N, S-CDs as fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(e) displays the fluorescence intensities at different concentration of Hg2+ ions. As the Hg2+ concentration increased from 0.01 to 60 μM, fluorescence intensity gradually debilitated. The strong linear relationship exists (y = 1.1682x + 0.0859) between fluorescence quenching and Hg2+ concentration in the concentration range 0.01‑60 μM with regression coefficient as 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated to be 3.1 nM for Hg2+ using LOD = 3σ/S, where σ denotes standard deviation of the blank N, S-CDs samples (N = 10) and S is the slope of the regression equation [Fig. 4(f)]. The sensing performance of various fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ ions have been compared in Table 1 . It can be concluded that the synthesized CDs from patera, in comparison to previously reported probes, have an exceptional quantum yield, a wide linear range, and considerably low LOD, much lower than previously reported Hg2+ probes endorsing that these CDs provide a selective and sensitive platform for ultra-trace level assay of mercury ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of fluorescence signal of N, S-CDs using cysteine
Cysteine specificity of the CDs-Hg2+ system
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that amino acids like cysteine, l-methionine and histidine chelate strongly with Hg2+ ions due to the presence of functional groups [56]. Cysteine is a naturally occuring amine which contains thiol group and exhibit strong affinity for mercury ions, thus it was selected for recovering fluorescence signal. To evaluate the specificity of cysteine over other amino acids for recovering fluorescence signal, various amino acids including histidine, lysine, methionine, cysteine, arginine, glutathione, glycine and glutamine were verified for fluorescent turn ON response. It was observed that [Fig. 5(a)] cysteine gave the maximum recovery (i.e. 95 %) of original fluorescence signal of N,S-CDs owing to its existence as thiolate form (the formation constant of cysteine and Hg2+ is ≈1042), thus is a superior coordinating ligand with higher affinity towards Hg2+ ions than other naturally occurring thiols [57]. Moreover, the inclusion of coexisting amino acids did not interfere with the fluorescence of the CDs-Hg2+ system, as shown in Fig. 5(b). This can be explained because of the bonds generated between the thiol groups and heavy metal ions have been found to be significantly stronger than electrostatic interactions. As a result of the establishment of the Hg2+-SH bond, biothiol's binding to Hg2+ is stronger than Hg2+ binding to the CDs, which compromises the couplings between Hg2+ and the CDs, leading to fluorescence enhancement. After confirming the selectivity of the CDs-Hg2+ system towards cysteine, parameters such as pH and the incubation time were optimized. The incubation time of 2 min and pH 7 were concluded as the optimal sensing conditions for cysteine using N, S-CDs [Fig. 5(c) and (d), respectively].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N, S-CDs sensor towards cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensitivity of N, S-CDs towards cysteine, an aqueous solution prepared with the 15 μL of N, S-CDs as well as 60 μM Hg2+ in order to make sure that the system can detect cysteine quantitatively. The N, S-CDs solution was in a quenched condition owing to the presence of Hg2+, thus the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs − Hg2+ system increased with gradual addition of cysteine. The recovery of fluorescence was proportional to the concentration of cysteine as illustrated in Fig. 5(e) and (f). The FL intensity of the N, S-CDs − Hg2+ complex and Cys concentration can be depicted by a strong linear relationship (y = 3.7938x + 0.1855) in the region of 0.05–12.5 μM with regression coefficient as 0.995. The lowest detection limit for cysteine calculated using 3σ/S was 8 nM, indicating that it can be effectively used as a cysteine probe along with mercury ions. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs − Hg2+ as a fluorescence response towards cysteine was compared to other reported sensors as shown in Table 2 . It is evident from the table that Typha angustata Bory derived N, S-CDs have excellent potential as sensor for both Hg2+ ions and cysteine with extremely low level of detection not reported hitherto.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for detection of Hg2+ and cysteine in “on-off-on” mode and fluorescence lifetime studies
ce:section.ce:para: The external abundance of carboxyl, hydroxyl, CS and CN groups make most N, S-CDs susceptible to metal ions. The intensity of the fluorescence emission was significantly quenched when Hg2+ ions were added to the synthesized N, S-CDs aqueous solution. The generation of N, S-CDs − Hg2+ complexes are probably responsible for the substantial quenching effect. According to the previous studies, the intramolecular photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process from excited N, S-CDs − Hg2+ ions is a plausible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs [Fig. 6(a)] [61]. When Hg2+ ions were introduced, the electron-deficient Hg2+ complexes with functional groups on the N, S-CDs (for example, carboxyl groups, thiol groups and amino groups,) caused the d-orbital of Hg2+ to split. Consequently, electrons in the excited state of N, S-CDs were partly transported to the Hg2+ d-orbital. Fluorescence quenching occurred as a result of the electron transition in the radiation forms (FL emission) being restricted. The excited state of N, S-CDs may relax its energy in a non-electron transfer way upon photo-excitation, resulting in a significant drop in N, S-CDs fluorescence, as seen in Fig. 6(b). N, S-CDs solution exhibits strong blue fluorescence when Hg2+ ions are introduced to this solution, virtually quenching the fluorescence signal completely.

The quenched fluorescence of N, S-CDs is regained promptly after the addition of cysteine to the N, S-CDs − Hg2+ solution. This might be attributed to the fact that the interaction between Hg2+ and the sulfhydryl of the cysteine molecule is greater than the Hg2+ S bond. N, S-CDs owing to its existence as thiolate form (the formation constant of cysteine and Hg2+ is ≈1042), thus is a superior coordinating ligand with higher affinity towards Hg2+ ions than other naturally occurring thiols. The bonds generated between the thiol groups and heavy metal ions have been found to be significantly stronger than electrostatic interactions. As a result of the establishment of the Hg2+ SH bond, biothiol's binding to Hg2+ is stronger than Hg2+ binding to the N, S-CDs, which compromises the couplings between Hg2+ and the N, S-CDs, leading to fluorescence enhancement with addition of cysteine.

To demonstrate the aforesaid principle, UV–vis spectroscopy was employed. In comparison to N, S-CDs absorption at 320 nm [black line in Fig. 6(c)], the absorbance peak for the FL quenched N, S-CDs − Hg2+ system (red solid) exhibits a small red-shift at 325 nm indicating that Hg2+ forms a complex with the N/S-CDs, whereas the absorption peak position of the quenched N, S-CDs − Hg2+–Cys system (blue line) reverted to its former location when cysteine was introduced, indicating that Hg2+ ions were removed from N, S-CDs' surface. In the wavelength region of 200 to 300 nm, no noticeable absorbance for Hg2+ ions, cysteine, or Hg2+-Cys was seen throughout the experiment, therefore this small red-shift could be ascribed to interactions between Hg2+ ions and functional groups on the N, S-CDs surface [62,63]. Single photon counting methods were used to record fluorescence lifetimes, and the obtained FL decay curves show multi-exponential function behavior as shown in Fig. 6 (c). For the aqueous N, S-CDs solution, the fluorescence decay curve was fitted to obtain lifetimes of 2.01 ns (χ2 = 1.44) with two components 1.23 ns (52.25 %) and 7.10 ns (47.7), 1.94 ns and 1.97 ns, respectively. The lifetime remained unchanged when Hg2+ and cysteine were introduced (see Table 3 ). These results demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching in these processes is static [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility and recyclability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reversibility of the indicator is significant for recycling and comprehending the sensor mechanism [3]. The consecutive addition of consistent quantities of Hg2+ ions (60 μM) and cysteine (12.5 μM) to the aqueous solution of N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs + Hg2+ complex, respectively result in an alternate variation in fluorescence intensity. According to the original fluorescence value of N, S-CDs, the fluorescence recovery and quenching efficiency reached as high as 95 % after 10 cycles, exhibiting excellent “on–off–on” reversibility, as shown in Fig. 6(d &e).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to assess the composition of the synthesized N, S–CDs and changes that take place on complexation with Hg2+ ions. The full scan XPS spectrum of the N, S–CDs, as shown in Fig. 7(a), presents four peaks at 531.13, 400.7, 285.8, and 164.8 eV, which correspond to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and S 2p, respectively. Elemental analysis reveals that N, S–CDs contained 54.81 % C, 24.24 % O, 15.43 % N and 5.51 % S. This indicated that N and S have been successfully doped into the framework. The deconvolutions of high-resolution XPS data of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p are shown in Fig. 7(c–f). The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum [Fig. 7(c) (i)] of N, S-CDs reveal the presence of four peaks corresponding to CC/CC, CS, CN and CO groups at 284.2, 285.1, 286.3, and 288.7 eV [65]. In case of nitrogen spectrum [Fig. 7(d) (i)], two peaks at 399.1 and 400.9 eV were assigned to the presence of CN and NH bonds [53,66]. Additionally, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum [Fig. 7(e) (i)] revealed two peaks at 531.8, 532.3 eV due to the presence of CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [53]. The high resolution spectrum of S 2p [Fig. 7(f) (i)] exhibited four major sub peaks which are attributed to –CSC 2p3/2 (163.5 eV), CSC 2p1/2 (164.2 eV), S = O (168.7 eV) and CSOx , x = 2, 3, 4 (169.4 eV) groups respectively [65]. These results indicate that the surface of N, SCDs is functionalized with amino, hydroxyl, carboxylic/carbonyl and sulfides moieties derived from thiourea, which is consistent with the FTIR data. The survey scan [Fig. 7(a)] clearly reveals that in addition to the peak for carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur a new peak appeared at 101.5 eV corresponds to Hg2+ ions. Fig. 7(b) shows that significant signals at 101.5 eV and 104 eV may be attributed to Hg 4f7/2 and Hg 4f5/2, respectively.

The synthesized N, S-CDs were then used to assess Hg2+ ions and cys in real water samples such as tap water and lake water. For spiked water samples, the standard addition method protocol with two spiked levels (0.5 μΜ and 20 μΜ) for Hg2+ ions and (0.3 μΜ and, 9.5 μΜ) for cys were used. Water samples were taken from a tap, Panjab University and Sukhna Lake, Chandigarh. To remove impurities, the collected water samples were filtered with 0.22 μm qualitative filter paper and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min before the experiments. The unknown concentration evaluated from the regression line in Fig. 4(f) for Hg2+ ions and Fig. 5(f) for cys demonstrated an excellent conformity between the measured concentrations and the spiked concentrations. Recovery levels for the spiked river water samples range from 95 to 102 % for Hg2+ ions and 97 to 104 % for cys, respectively as shown in Table 4 . The method demonstrates high reliability and accuracy for Hg2+ ions and cys detection in real samples.

Highly fluorescent N, S-CDs were synthesized employing a wild plant, Typha angustata Bory (Patera) as a novel carbon precursor by a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms significantly promoted the electron transport and coordination in the interaction of N, S-CDs with Hg2+. Hg2+ ions significantly quenched the fluorescence signal of N, S-CDs, however, addition of cysteine into N, S-CDs + Hg2+ complex could recover the quenched fluorescence signal. Consequently, N, S-CDs were discovered to exhibit great sensitivity and ion selectivity when employed as a fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+and Cys at extremely low detection limits of 3.1 nM and 8 nM, respectively. Excellent reversibility up to 10 cycles with mere 5 % loss of fluorescence signal was demonstrated for the designed on–off–on system. Additionally, the synthesized N, S-CDs have been shown to be reliable and practicable for detecting Hg2+ in real water samples with recovery rate ranging from 95 to 102 % and 97 to 104 % for Hg2+ and cys, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime decay results exhibited a very rapid response time of 2.01 ns confirming the suitability of samples for mercury ions and cysteine. Typha angustata Bory (Patera) derived N, S-CDs and the designed on–off–on sensor strategy are expected to benefit from the aforementioned advantages and have potential applications in environmental remediation.
